1: שִׁמְעָה אֱלֹהִים רִנָּתִי (shîmʿāh ʾĕlōhîm rinnātî, "Hear my cry, O God!") — rinnāh: the ringing cry (originally of joy, but in lament psalms: the urgent, piercing cry that demands to be heard — Ps 17:1; 142:6). The difference between tĕpillāh (a measured prayer) and rinnāh (a piercing cry): this is the latter. הַקְשִׁיבָה תְּפִלָּתִי (haqshîbāh tĕpillātî, "listen to my prayer") — qāshab (hiphil: to pay attention, to prick up the ears): the two-fold plea: hear the cry and attend to the prayer.
2: מִקְצֵה הָאָרֶץ אֵלֶיךָ אֶקְרָא (miqqĕṣeh hāʾāreṣ ʾêlêkā ʾeqrāʾ, "From the ends of the earth I call out to you") — qāṣeh: the end, the edge, the furthest extremity. The prayer of the exile — one who is at the world's edge, removed from Jerusalem, distant from the temple. The ends of the earth as both geographical distance and spiritual extremity. בַּעֲטֹף לִבִּי (baʿăṭōp libbî, "when my heart grows faint") — ʿāṭap: to grow faint, to languish (as in Ps 142:3 — the spirit overwhelmed). בְּצוּר-יָרוּם מִמֶּנִּי תַנְחֵנִי (bĕṣûr-yārûm mimmennî tanḥênî, "lead me to the rock that is higher than I") — ṣûr yārûm mimmennî: "a rock that is too high for me" — the inaccessible cliff, higher than unaided human climbing can reach. Only the divine guide (nāḥāh: to lead) can take the Psalmist to the rock that is beyond human reach. The prayer is not to climb alone but to be led.
3: כִּי-הָיִיתָ מַחְסֶה לִי (kî-hāyîtā maḥseh lî, "For you have been a shelter for me") — maḥseh: a refuge, a place of shelter (as in Ps 46:1 — maḥăseh wāʿōz). The past experience of divine shelter grounds the present plea. מִגְדַּל-עֹז מִפְּנֵי אוֹיֵב (migdal-ʿōz mippĕnê ʾôyêb, "a strong tower against the enemy") — migdal: a tower (the military watchtower — Prov 18:10: "The name of the LORD is a strong tower; the righteous run into it and are safe"). ʿŌz: strength. The combined image: God as both the low-lying shelter (maḥseh) and the tall strong tower (migdal ʿōz).
4: אָגוּרָה בְאָהָלְךָ עוֹלָמִים (ʾāgûrāh bĕʾāhālĕkā ʿôlāmîm, "I will dwell in your tent forever") — gûr: to sojourn, to live as a temporary resident (the alien/pilgrim's residency — but here: the permanent dwelling of the covenant member in the divine tent). ʿÔlāmîm: the plural intensification of "forever" — across all the ages. אֶחְסֶה בְסֵתֶר כְּנָפֶיךָ (ʾeḥseh bĕsêter kĕnāpêkā, "I will take refuge under the shelter of your wings") — sêter kĕnāpêkā: "the secret place of your wings" — the hiding in the inner shadow of the divine wings (as in Ps 27:5; 31:20 — the "shelter" / sûkkāh of divine protection). Selāh.
5: כִּי-אַתָּה אֱלֹהִים שָׁמַעְתָּ לִנְדָרָי (kî-ʾattāh ʾĕlōhîm shāmaʿtā lîndāray, "For you, O God, have heard my vows") — neder: the vow (the binding promise made in crisis, as in Ps 56:12). נָתַתָּ יְרֻשַּׁת יִרְאֵי שְׁמֶךָ (nātatā yĕrushshat yirʾê shĕmekā, "you have given me the inheritance of those who fear your name") — yĕrushshāh: inheritance, possession (from yārash: to possess, to inherit — the Conquest vocabulary). The inheritance is the possession of those who fear (yārēʾ) the divine name — the covenant community's common inheritance.
6: יָמִים עַל-יְמֵי-מֶלֶךְ תּוֹסִיף (yāmîm ʿal-yĕmê-melek tôsîp, "You will extend the king's days") — the psalm's center of gravity shifts to the king (perhaps the Psalmist himself in his royal capacity, or a priestly prayer for the reigning king). Tôsîp (hiphil: to add, to increase): God adding days to the royal life. שְׁנוֹתָיו כְּמוֹ-דֹר וָדֹר (shĕnôtāyw kĕmô-dōr wādōr, "his years shall span many generations") — dōr wādōr: "generation after generation" — the hyperbolic prayer for the royal life extended across multiple human generations. This exceeds any historical king's lifespan — pointing toward the eschatological king whose "years endure through all generations" (Ps 102:24). The NT reads this as the eternal life of Christ the King.
7: יֵשֵׁב עוֹלָם לִפְנֵי אֱלֹהִים (yêshêb ʿôlām lipnê ʾĕlōhîm, "May he dwell before God forever") — yāshab lipnê ʾĕlōhîm: to sit/dwell before God (the posture of covenant access and royal authority — as in Ps 41:12: "you place me before your face forever"). ʿÔlām: forever. חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת מַן יִנְצְרֻהוּ (ḥesed weʾĕmet man yinṣĕrûhû, "appoint steadfast love and faithfulness to guard him") — ḥesed and ʾĕmet personified as appointed guards (nāṣar: to keep, to guard — the sentinel's task). The same twin divine attributes that are sent as envoys in Ps 57:3 are here assigned as the king's personal bodyguard. The prayer: may God's own covenant-loyalty and truth stand watch over the king. In the Messianic reading, these are the attributes of the divine Son who is guarded by the Father's eternal character.
8: כֵּן אֲזַמְּרָה שִׁמְךָ לָעַד (kên ʾăzammĕrāh shimkā lāʿad, "So I will sing praise to your name forever") — lāʿad: forever (a stronger form: "to the perpetuity"). The Psalmist's vow: the perpetual praise of the divine shêm (name — the whole person and character of God) in response to the divine keeping promised in v7. לְשַׁלְּמִי נְדָרַי יוֹם יוֹם (lĕshallĕmî nĕdāray yôm yôm, "as I fulfill my vows day after day") — shālam (piel: to complete, to pay in full — the wholeness-root); yôm yôm: day after day — the daily rhythm of vow-fulfillment. The psalm that began from the ends of the earth ends in the daily round of praise — exile transformed into liturgy, extremity into regularity.
